perturbation.original_text: Philips invoices, compliant with the requirements of this Agreement, will be payable within [Redacted - Commercially Sensitive] following the end of the month of the date of invoice. Customer shall make all payments in Euros by check, wire transfer or automated clearing house to the bank account designated by Philips.
perturbation.changed_text: Philips invoices, compliant with the requirements of this Agreement, will be payable within [Redacted - Commercially Sensitive] following the end of the month of the date of invoice. However, if Customer pays within 15 days of the invoice date, Customer shall make all payments in Euros by check, wire transfer or automated clearing house to the bank account designated by Philips.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction in payment terms. The original text states that invoices are payable within a specific timeframe after the end of the month of the invoice date, providing one clear deadline. The modified text adds a condition: If the Customer pays within 15 days of the invoice date, the original payment schedule applies. This creates confusion about when payment is actually due and whether the original payment timeframe is altered by the incentive for early payment. This in-text contradiction makes it unclear which payment terms are to be enforced, which can lead to legal dispute. The condition incentivizing early payment creates ambiguity regarding the actual due date for invoices.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Philips invoices, compliant with the requirements of this Agreement, will be payable within [Redacted - Commercially Sensitive] following the end of the month of the date of invoice. However, if Customer pays within 15 days of the invoice date, Customer shall make all payments in Euros by check, wire transfer or automated clearing house to the bank account designated by Philips.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradictory element regarding payment terms. The original text and contradicted text both state that invoices are payable within [Redacted - Commercially Sensitive] following the end of the month of the invoice date, which provides a specific deadline. The modified text adds 'However, if Customer pays within 15 days of the invoice date,' creating a condition that suggests an alternate, potentially conflicting, payment arrangement. This introduces ambiguity and conflicts with the original single deadline.

perturbation.original_text: Customer may not cancel a previously accepted Purchase Order .
perturbation.changed_text: Customer may cancel a previously accepted Purchase Order, subject to a cancellation fee of 10% of the total order value, if cancellation is made more than 30 days before the confirmed delivery date. Customer may not cancel a previously accepted Purchase Order.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly prohibits the cancellation of previously accepted Purchase Orders by the Customer. By inserting the phrase “Customer may cancel a previously accepted Purchase Order, subject to a cancellation fee of 10% of the total order value, if cancellation is made more than 30 days before the confirmed delivery date.”, and retaining the original, contradictory statement, an in-text contradiction has been created. This makes the contract ambiguous and creates uncertainty as to the Customer's right to cancel orders, and it is unclear whether cancellation is ever permitted. The contradiction creates uncertainty regarding the enforceability of either clause.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Philips shall confirm to Customer the receipt of each Purchase Order issued hereunder (each, a "Confirmation") within seven (7) days following Philips's receipt thereof in writing or in any other mutually agreed manner of communication (e.g., EDI or email). Each Confirmation must reference Customer's Purchase Order number, confirm acceptance of the Purchase Order, include a confirmed date of Delivery (which may differ from the requested one in the Purchase Order) or, solely if permitted under this clause 4.4, advise Customer of Philips's rejection of such Purchase Order, the date of acceptance or rejection and the basis for rejection, if applicable. If Philips commences performance under such Purchase Order, Philips will be deemed to have accepted the Purchase Order. Customer may withdraw any Purchase Order prior to Philips' acceptance thereof. Philips may only reject a Purchase Order if (a) the quantity ordered by Customer in such Purchase Order is inconsistent with the quantity in the applicable Forecast in accordance with clause 3.2 (as amended pursuant to clause 3.4), (b) Philips has sent Customer a Notice of termination pursuant to clause 12 or (c) the applicable Purchase Order includes terms and conditions that supplement those contained in this Agreement, which Philips is unwilling to accept. Philips may not cancel any previously accepted Purchase Order hereunder. Customer may cancel a previously accepted Purchase Order, subject to a cancellation fee of 10% of the total order value, if cancellation is made more than 30 days before the confirmed delivery date. Customer may not cancel a previously accepted Purchase Order .
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a direct contradiction. The original text states unequivocally that "Customer may not cancel a previously accepted Purchase Order." The changed text adds a clause allowing cancellation under specific conditions, while retaining the original prohibition, creating an irreconcilable inconsistency. This contradiction occurs within the same paragraph of the contradicted text, specifically stating that 'Customer may cancel a previously accepted Purchase Order, subject to a cancellation fee of 10% of the total order value, if cancellation is made more than 30 days before the confirmed delivery date. Customer may not cancel a previously accepted Purchase Order'. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction', where 'different sections of a contract provide conflicting deadlines, obligations, or penalties'.

perturbation.original_text: Customer will implement such recall campaign at Customer's sole cost and risk. Customer hereby covenants and agrees that it shall bear all costs and expenses related to the implementation of any such recall.
perturbation.changed_text: Customer will implement such recall campaign at Customer's sole cost and risk, except that Philips shall reimburse Customer for the labor costs associated with the recall. Customer hereby covenants and agrees that it shall bear all costs and expenses related to the implementation of any such recall.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause states that Customer bears the entire cost and risk of a recall campaign. The modified clause creates a contradiction by adding an exception that Philips will reimburse Customer for labor costs. The final sentence, which reaffirms Customer's responsibility for all costs, further emphasizes the contradiction. This creates uncertainty regarding the financial responsibility for recall campaigns.
perturbation.contradicted_text: If Customer or any relevant Governmental Authority determines that a recall campaign is necessary, Customer will implement such recall campaign at Customer's sole cost and risk, except that Philips shall reimburse Customer for the labor costs associated with the recall. Customer hereby covenants and agrees that it shall bear all costs and expenses related to the implementation of any such recall. In case of a recall of the Product, Philips shall at its sole cost provide full cooperation to Customer in order to achieve an efficient and effective recall by Customer. Philips shall provide such cooperation at its own costs, including internal organizational costs but not including the costs of repair, replacement, installation of Products and logistics related to the recall [Redacted - Commercially Sensitive]. For greater certainty, the obligations of the Parties pursuant to this clause 7.6 shall only apply in respect of Products sold or distributed on or after the Effective Date, and for clarity, to thwe extent there is a recall that involves any Product sold or distributed prior to the Effective Date, the covenants and obligations of the Parties pursuant to this clause 7.6 shall not apply (and shall be addressed by the terms of the Purchase Agreement).
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception where Philips reimburses Customer for labor costs associated with a recall. However, the subsequent sentence and the contradicted text state that the Customer shall bear all costs and expenses related to the implementation of any recall. This creates a direct conflict regarding who is responsible for labor costs during a recall, creating a strong in-text contradiction as defined by the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' perturbation type.
